author: @idea-shack
text: Have you considered connecting the coils and electrolytic caps as a LC resonant circuit for each one (without diodes), that way they will oscillate and the oscillations will 'load' the coils for you. If you only drain a small fraction of the energy of the oscillation (not the whole lot), then you have these oscillations multiplying the generation capacity of your setup so that the lack iron core or strong magnets becomes irrelevant.

replies.author: @idea-shack
replies.text: On second thought, you would have to use a diode to protect the electrolytic cap against reverse polarity during oscillations, but you could use two electrolytic caps in reverse with diodes so that the caps charge and discharge only in half waves where their polarity is correctly connected. You will have to aim for a natural frequency roughly matching some gentle spin of your turbine per second multiplied by the number of magnets you have divided by 2 (I think). If you use a mosfet that only turns on for some fraction of the peak of the oscillations, then you can bleed off only a portion of the energy for a load and ensure that some always remains to keep the oscillation going and generator working properly. Also you can connect every other coil in series with each other and then only use one or two large capacitors, no need to do it for each coil, simplifies things.

author: @edkoetsier339
text: Er.. Strength of magnetic flux is directly proportional to the emf produced. Blame Faraday. The very best ceramic magnets are very good, and produce ~1/2 of the Gauss that N40 NdFeB produce. But very cheap (and the most common) ceramic magnets are Very weak, and only produce in the range of 1/10th to 2/10th the Gauss of a garden variety rare earth magnet. So, yes, if you have very good ceramic magnets, you will have a good result. You would have a great result with N40's of the same size, but it would cost a bit more. Young players beware, you only get what you pay for....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's true mate but it would only work to a point and there are the opposing effects to consider

replies.author: @edkoetsier339
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering As in all things, but I were designing it, I would use 2 rotors of cheap NdFeB magnets and carefully wound magnet copper coils. However this design would never have occurred to me, and it's not my design so I'll shut up. But please don't denigrate any technology over any other unless it deserves it. NdFeB magnets always have more Gauss, so will always be better in any wind generator, though a bit more expensive.

author: @garethb5729
text:
You on the right path with your magnet choice. 
The stronger the magnet, the smaller the field. 
A coil will pick up a ferrite magnet from a few centimetres yet the same coil would have to be a few millimetres from the Neyodynium magnets.
You may consider some iron for you coils tho. The induction is effected by the magnetic permeability of the core. 

To combat the cogging effect of iron cores. Space 3 coils across 4 poles.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
These equasions might help predict a generator's performance under load.

Open circuit voltage = RPM × (the generator's Kv motor constant, volts/rpm)

But, under resistive load these equasions are helpfull. 
current in a series circuit is the same everywhere in that series circuit.  and 
i=v/r; thus 
1) Current = (open circuit voltage) × (coils resistance + load resistance ); and 
v=i×r
2) voltage across a load  = current through that load × resistance  in that load. 

Thus, it works out that for a given rpm, the voltage across the load (coil's output voltage under load) = (open circuit voltage) × (load resistance)/ (load resistance + coil's resistance),  and the difference between the open circuit voltage and the output voltage is the voltage drop across the coil's internal resistance. 

thus one need only the open circuit voltage per rpm, the rpm, resistance  in the coil and resistance  in the load.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: One of the challenges with VAWTs and direct drive is getting enough rate of change in magnetic fields to get usefull electricity for charging a battery.  That's why some might consider neodymium magnets.  The when using ceramic magnets one needs to spend more on turns of copper coils with the current limiting voltage dropping internal resistance that comes with longer and thinner wire.

author: @mrdewilliams
text:
You are absolutely right on neodymium magnets vs ceramic.  If you look at what magnets are used in permanent magnet alternators for automobiles, they are ceramic.  If you look at most generators on the market, they are using ceramic magnets.  Of all my searching, I only found one reference to neodymium magnets being used on a wind turbine generator.

If you think about it, it makes perfect sense.  On a generator, the only drag (friction) you want is from the force that is turning your mechanism.  You try to reduce drag everywhere else.  Having stronger magnets might give you a stronger magnetic field to pass through your coils, but the increased current will be very small compared to the increased drag caused by the repulsion created by the magnetic field from in the coil.  People tend to forget that when you create current in a coil, that current is moving through the coil and creating a magnetic fields of its own (Lenz's law).  So the trade-off between the increased current generated eventually becomes offset by the increased drag caused by countering magnetic fields.  That doesn't even take cost of materials compared to the perceived gains at all.

Where is that trade-off point?  I couldn't say.  I am an amateur when it comes to this stuff.  But, considering that the professionals that engineer these things for commercial production favor ceramic over neodymium when it comes to motors and generators, I think I know which side of the argument I would choose to fall on.

It amazes me, the things people get worked up about. What purpose does it serve anybody to find fault with your approach, provided you’re not hurting anyone. 
Personally, I think we waste opportunity in our lives when we are constantly looking for the perfect, technically superior solution to a challenge. Those parameters take us to the conclusion that the only solution worth pursuing must involve spending a fortune on the latest-greatest, mass produced product. So people don’t even try to find a DIY solution. What a pity. And what a lost opportunity. 
In any case, I keenly enjoy watching your videos. Keep up the good (and important) work.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: One wants to keep the air gap between magnets and coils as small as possible.  The magnetic equivalent of electrical resistance is ‘reluctance’ and the reluctance of air is very high. This is why a car alternator is only about 40% efficient (due to large machining tolerances) but power station generators are engineered to achieve 90%. Magnetic fields decay as the inverse ‘cube’ of the distance , so the size of the gap has a very significant effect.

replies.author: @kuhrd
replies.text: Standard car alternators are typically around 55-60% efficient but it depends on the speed of the motor and the load being drawn so at slower engine speeds they tend to be less efficient.  Most of the losses are coming from the internal heating of the windings themselves due to resistance and inductance at the high current draw.  A typical car or truck alternator may be putting out 125-300 Amps at 14-15VDC so you are asking that tiny alternator to output up to 1750-4500 watts at maximum load.   High-efficiency alternators are typically in the 68-75% range. The gap also does play a big role but that is mainly needed because most alternators need to function in a harsh environment where dust, dirt, and debris can enter the unit thus tolerances in some cases must be larger.

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text: @kuhrd Used to work for Lucas CAV. The OEM’s (our customers) wanted automotive alternators  as inexpensive as possible — so hence the wide tolerances (there are four surfaces for which ‘compound error’ can increase or decrease the air gap and to each there is a associated cost). Even our EA sealed alternators (those used in explosive atmospheres like coal mines and oil refineries) where no crud can enter, had the same wide tolerances for reasons of cost. Also I was talking real world. Cars are geared so that the average driving rpm is around 2,000 rpm at which speed the alternator is only 35 – 40% efficient. For wind turbines, where the alternator speed may  only be a 1,000 rpm, then one is looking at an efficiency of about 5%. A magneto would be more appropriate for these low speeds, such as Rob is building.  Also, as Rob alluded to — faster is better.  It is also preferable to choose the right wire gauge etc., to suit the operating perimeters of wind instillation but for this type of experimentation, Rob is making the best of what is just laying around (my definition of ‘rubbish’ is some thing useful, in the wrong place at the wrong time). This is one of the problems with degree courses. All theory, with very little in the way of awakening innate creativity. This channel certainly helps fill that gap.

For a low wind speed, how about using two of these mounted on the same shaft with one rotating clockwise with magnets on the bottom and another below it designed with the rotor to go counter clockwise with coils on the top of that one.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Most revolutionary inovations within a particular field of study at made by people who are not indoctrinated within that field of study as they don't know what's not possible.  

This goes back to the psychological studies performed by Goethe.  After awhile being emersed in a subject, the mind literally can't see anything outside the set parameters of that field.  Hence you have a lot of innovation coming from folks in the beginning of their career, and then they spend the rest of their career refining those ideas, but they don't really come up with anything new after that.  The same holds true when people switch fields later in life.

True, people can come up with stupid ideas too. Einstein came up with stupid ideas.  The trick, as he put it, was to keep coming up with ideas until one of them hit.

author: @pulesjet
text: If you are in fact looking for neodymium magnets it is actually cheaper to by a hover board hub motor then the magnets alone.  They pop right out with a bit of care.   They can be had for about a dollar each.

replies.author: @popuptoaster
replies.text: I'm using one of those hub motors in my DIY wind turbine, nearly gave myself a hernia trying to separate the two halves until I worked out it's MUCH easier if you leave the face plate on to keep the spindle centralised. 🤣

author: @pulesjet
text: I purchased 120 ceramic  magnets and was quite surprised at just how good they turned out.  They will find a home.

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: Try stacking them in a pyramid;)

author: @mikebond6328
text: I bet you get a better output from this one now with the ferrous metal behind the magnets.

author: @glennwebster1675
text: Looking Awsome.. I have learned quite a bit from your videos... Thank you.
